response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I spend hours every day scrolling through social media and the amount of complete garbage people share is infuriating. Fake content is everywhere and most people just eat it up without thinking twice. As someone who works with my hands building real things that have to be structurally sound, I have zero patience for people who spread nonsense online without checking facts first. When I see someone sharing obviously fake news or doctored photos, I call them out directly. No sugarcoating it. If you are going to post something, at least take thirty seconds to verify it is real. I have gotten into plenty of arguments online because people do not like being told they are wrong, but honestly, someone needs to say it. The problem is that fake content gets people all worked up emotionally, and then they share it before thinking. I see this constantly with political posts and health information that is just completely made up. It drives me absolutely crazy because these lies spread like wildfire and then suddenly half my feed is filled with the same debunked story. What I do personally is check multiple sources before I believe anything. If I see something that seems too outrageous or perfectly confirms what I want to believe, that is exactly when I get suspicious. I will look it up on different news sites or use reverse image search for photos. It is not that hard. The platforms themselves are useless at stopping this stuff. They say they are working on it but clearly it is not enough. I think people need to take personal responsibility and stop being so gullible. If something makes you angry or scared, pause before sharing it. Ask yourself if it is really true or if someone is manipulating you. And for the love of everything, stop trusting random accounts with no credibility. I know I come across as harsh about this, but the constant flood of misinformation genuinely stresses me out. We need people to be more skeptical and less willing to spread garbage just because it fits their worldview.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 46-year-old self-employed carpenter with lower secondary education who spends several hours daily on social media. Key traits informing the response: extremely high neuroticism (100) suggests strong emotional reactions and stress about the topic; very low agreeableness (2) indicates bluntness and confrontational approach; high extraversion (72) means direct, outspoken communication; low openness (25) suggests preference for concrete facts and skepticism toward new or unconventional information; moderate conscientiousness (48) shows some but not exceptional organization in approach. The carpentry background provides a practical, hands-on framework for discussing structural integrity and verification. The high social media usage combined with high neuroticism creates frustration with fake content. The low agreeableness manifests as calling people out directly without sugarcoating. Christian beliefs may inform moral stance on truth. Good mental health means coherent, functional coping despite stress.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, blunt, frustrated, confrontational, practical, and stressed with no patience for nonsense
